data.title: Compass to acquire Christie's International Real Estate, @properties, Ansley
data.description:
Compass Inc. (NYSE:COMP) agreed to acquire Christie's International Real Estate, @properties, and Ansley Real Estate for $150M in cash and 44.1M shares of Compass class A common stock, the company said Monday. That values the acquisition at ~$463M, based on COMP's closing price of $7.09 on Nov. 29, 2024.

The price may be adjusted, depending on the price of Compass shares a year after the transaction closes.

The transaction will accelerate Compass's domestic and international growth opportunities, the company said in a statement. It will get global reach through Christie's International Real Estate's network of 100+ independently owned domestic and international Affiliates across 50 countries and territories. In addition, @properties is the eighth largest residential brokerage in the United States by sales volume, with operations in Greater Chicago, Indiana, Michigan, Wisconsin, and Metro Atlanta (through the Ansley Real Estate Brand).

Compass (NYSE:COMP) stock rose 2.9% in after-hours trading.

If Compass (COMP) fails to obtain antitrust clearance for the deal, it may be required to pay Christie's International Real Estate a termination fee of $22.25M.

"This partnership will allow us to empower strong independent brokerages and broker-owner entrepreneurs around the world who are Affiliates under Christie's International Real Estate," said Robert Reffkin, Compass (COMP) founder and CEO.

The deal will also include title and mortgage businesses and a lead-generation business, in line with Compass's (COMP) long-term strategy to add high-margin services to its portfolio.

data.title: Zscaler drops despite solid Q1 results, Q2 EPS 1 cent shy of estimate
data.description:
Zscaler (NASDAQ:ZS) slipped during early post-market trading on Monday after it released first quarter fiscal 2025 financial results that surpassed market estimates.

For the quarter ended October 31, the cloud-based cybersecurity platform reported non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.77 versus the consensus estimate of $0.63. It reported revenue of $628M, which was more than the consensus of $605.55M.

Revenue for the quarter grew 26% year over year, while calculated billings increased 13%.

Looking ahead, second quarter adjusted earnings per share were a penny short of expectations.

For the quarter in progress, Zscaler expects revenue ranging from $633M to $635M, with a midpoint just above the consensus of $633.8M. It expects adjusted earnings per share of $0.68 to $0.69, which is just below the market estimate of $0.70.

Zscaler increased it full-year fiscal 2025 revenue to range from $2.62B to $2.64B, versus its prior forecast of $2.6B to $2.62B.

"The combination of Zero Trust and AI is creating exciting new opportunities, which we are well positioned to capture with our large and expanding platform," said CEO Jay Chaudhry. "With our customer obsession, the world’s largest cybersecurity cloud, and an upleveled go-to-market machine, we are driving strong growth."

Zscaler also highlighted recent integrations with the cybersecurity firms CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Okta (OKTA), and the appointment of Adam Geller as chief product officer. Geller formerly helped Palo Alto Networks (PANW) launch and expand multiple product lines before working at SIEM and Exabeam.

data.title: Credo Technology Non-GAAP EPS of $0.07 beats by $0.02, revenue of $72.03M beats by $5.22M
data.description:

data.title: Hercules Capital renews multi-currency SMBC credit facility
data.description:
• Hercules Capital (NYSE:HTGC) renewed and upsized its existing $225M credit facility with Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation with an upsized credit facility under which Synovus Bank, Customers Bank and Apple Bank, together with SMBC, have committed a total of $300M in credit capacity subject to borrowing base, leverage and other restrictions.
  • The renewed credit facility also includes an uncommitted accordion feature expandable up to $500.0 million.
  • The renewed credit facility matures in November 2029, including a 12-month amortization period.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Credo Technology surges as Q2 results, guidance top expectations
data.description:
Credo Technology (NASDAQ:CRDO) shares surged 28% in late trading on Monday after the technology company reported fiscal second-quarter results and guidance that blew past expectations.

For the period ending November 2, Credo, which makes connectivity solutions for optical and Ethernet applications, said it earned an adjusted $0.07 per share as revenue rose 64% year-over-year to come in at $72M. Analysts were expecting adjusted earnings of $0.07 per share on $66.5M in revenue.

“For the past few quarters, we have anticipated an inflection point in our revenues during the second half of fiscal 2025,” Bill Brennan, Credo’s President and Chief Executive Officer, stated. “I am pleased to share that this turning point has arrived, and we are experiencing even greater demand than initially projected, driven by AI deployments and deepening customer relationships.”

Looking to the third-quarter, Credo expects revenue to be between $115M and $125M, with adjusted gross margins between 61% and 63%. Analysts were expecting $86.04M in revenue.

The company will host a conference call at 5 p.m. EST to discuss the results.

data.title: Zscaler Non-GAAP EPS of $0.77 beats by $0.14, revenue of $627.96M beats by $22.35M
data.description:
• Zscaler press release (NASDAQ:ZS): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.77 beats by $0.14.
  • Revenue of $627.96M (+26.4% Y/Y) beats by $22.35M.
  • Shares -7.48%.
  • Calculated billings grows 13% year-over-year to $516.7 million
  • Deferred revenue grows 27% year-over-year to $1,783.7 million
  • GAAP net loss of $12.1 million compared to GAAP net loss of $33.5 million on a year-over-year basis
  • Non-GAAP net income of $124.3 million compared to non-GAAP net income of $86.4 million on a year-over-year basis

  • Financial Outlook

    For the second quarter of fiscal 2025, we expect:

    • Revenue of $633 million to $635 million, vs. $633.13M consensus
    • Non-GAAP income from operations of $126 million to $128 million
    • Non-GAAP net income per share of approximately $0.68 to $0.69, vs. $0.68 consensus, assuming approximately 163 million fully diluted shares outstanding and a non-GAAP tax rate of 23%

    For the full year of fiscal 2025, we expect:

    • Revenue of approximately $2.623 billion to $2.643 billion, vs. $2.61B consensus
    • Calculated billings of $3.124 billion to $3.149 billion
    • Non-GAAP income from operations of $549 million to $559 million
    • Non-GAAP net income per share of $2.94 to $2.99, vs. $2.87 consensus, assuming approximately 164 million fully diluted shares outstanding and a non-GAAP tax rate of 23%

data.title: Aimfinity Investment Corp. I extends deadline for business combination to December 28
data.description:
• Aimfinity Investment Corp. I (NASDAQ:AIMAU) announced that, in order to extend the date by which the company must complete its initial business combination from November 28, 2024 to December 28, 2024, I-Fa Chang, manager of the sponsor of the Company, has deposited into its trust account $60,000 on November 27, 2024.
  • The company may extend on a monthly basis from April 28, 2024 until January 28, 2025 or such an earlier date as may be determined by its board to complete a business combination by depositing the Monthly Extension Payment for each month into the Trust Account.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Intel board should consider 'radical change' with new CEO: analysts
data.description:
Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) announcement that Pat Gelsinger stepped down from leading the semiconductor giant should be followed up with "radical change," some in the investment community believe.

"While INTC's underperformance during Gelsinger's tenure as CEO was worse than that of INTC's previous four CEOs, INTC's stock hasn't outperformed the [Philadelphia Semiconductor Index] under any of them and only under Brian Krzanich's leadership did INTC beat the S&P 500," Truist analyst William Stein wrote in a note to clients. "We credit Gelsinger for making some significant changes at the company, but in our view he didn't move fast enough or go far enough."

Prior to Gelsinger, Intel was led by Craig Barrett from 1997 to 2005; Paul Otellini from 2005 to 2013; Brian Krzanich from 2013 to 2019; and Bob Swan from 2019 to 2021.

Problems go back decades

Intel's challenges go back decades and while the company has kept up its strength in manufacturing, that dominance led to a "culture of entitlement" that led to it missing out on seismic shifts in technology, including mobile and AI-centric compute, Stein added.

When the Santa Clara, Calif.-based Intel lost its manufacturing superiority to Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) around 2017, this ultimately revealed its "less-than-perfect" design choices, Stein added, as evidenced by the fact its revenue per employee is below competitors Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor, AMD (AMD) and GlobalFoundries (GFS).

"In retrospect, it all feels like Gelsinger tried to do too many things without clearly addressing the toughest thing: culture," Stein wrote. "This may have been a huge focus, but it certainly wasn't clear to us. Another CEO who tries to improve execution of Gelsinger's strategy seems unlikely, and we recall the challenges INTC had finding a CEO in each of the last two searches. We think INTC needs a more radical change."

Hamstrung?

Aside from Gelsinger's vision, of which the board may have lost confidence in, the company may be hamstrung via the U.S. CHIPs Act.

Last week, the Biden administration finalized a $7.86B grant to the company to boost domestic manufacturing, down from a previously announced $8.5B. However, there is a provision that prevents Intel from selling off its Intel Foundry Services unit and forces it to maintain a controlling stake.

This may have resulted in Gelsinger's "forced retirement," Seeking Alpha analyst Michael Del Monte said via email.

"The board cited that they have lost confidence in Mr. Gelsinger turning Intel around," Del Monte added.

New opportunities?

Intel's problems are not going to be fixed anytime soon, Mizuho analyst Vijay Rakesh said. It is a "distant third" to Nvidia and AMD in AI server adoption; the server CPU market is slowing and Intel is losing share to AMD; it has a nascent foundry business; and there are continued problems with AI ramps.

However, new leadership could give the company a new vision, even as these issues remain, Rakesh added. He kept his Neutral rating and $23 price target on Intel as it remains "as a key player in traditional PC/Server markets," even as it lags in AI.

data.title: ASML rises as company says new export curb control rules won't impact guidance
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) shares rose 3.5% on Monday as the company said the new semiconductor export curb control rules enacted by the Biden administration would not impact previously issued financial guidance for next year.

"For 2024, we do not expect any direct material impact on our business," ASML said in a statement. "For 2025, we expect that the impact will fall within what was communicated at the time of our Q3 2024 earnings, namely that we expect 2025 total net sales to be between €30 billion and €35 billion and that we expect our China business (net system sales plus net service and field option sales) to be around 20% of our total net sales for that full year."

ASML added that long-term demand scenarios will not be impacted by the new regulations, as these scenarios are based on global demand.

"We therefore also confirm potential 2030 scenarios for annual total net sales between approximately €44 billion and €60 billion as outlined during our Investor Day on November 14, 2024," ASML explained.

The semiconductor equipment firm also noted it is "fully committed" to complying with all applicable laws and regulations, including export control legislation in the countries it operates in.

Shares of other semiconductor equipment firms were also higher on Monday, including Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX) and KLA Corp. (KLAC).

Lam Research also said it does not expect the new export controls to impact previously issued guidance.

On Monday, the U.S. Department of Commerce placed additional curbs on China’s access to vital tools and components for AI chips. The new rules restrict sales of 27 types of high-bandwidth memory and chipmaking equipment, including products made outside the country by American companies, and are in addition to existing curbs affecting advanced logic chips.

The Chinese Ministry of Commerce called the U.S. restrictions “economic coercion,” and “seriously” threatening to the global supply chain. It said the country will take measures to safeguard its interests, but did not disclose what these measures would be.

Seeking Alpha's Preeti Singh contributed to this story.

(This story has been updated to include Lam Research's comment.)
